description: Curated read-only evaluator guidance covering web2 bug classes, advanced techniques, payload notes, and selected wordlist patterns. Methodology influenced by open-source bug bounty projects including https://github.com/shuvonsec/claude-bug-bounty (MIT); content is original authorship by the Hacker Bob contributors. This file is reference material only; execution remains in hacker-bob MCP tools and agents.

entries.title: REST/API authorization and parser differentials
techniques: Use two accounts for object access checks: replay attacker requests with victim IDs, tenant IDs, UUIDs, slugs, and indirect report/export IDs.
Compare sibling routes and methods. If GET is guarded, test PATCH/PUT/DELETE, batch endpoints, old API versions, and mobile/API variants for inconsistent auth.
Probe parser differentials with JSON vs form vs XML, duplicate JSON keys, repeated query params, and X-HTTP-Method-Override only where the endpoint already accepts writes.
Prioritize high-impact objects: billing, exports, invitations, admin actions, documents, messages, tokens, and organization membership.
payload_hints: PATCH profile with hidden fields: role, is_admin, verified, credits, permissions
Try version downgrade patterns: /api/v3/... -> /api/v2/... -> /api/v1/... -> /api/...
Try pagination abuse only when list endpoints expose sensitive objects: limit=999999, per_page=100000, count=-1
Check common discovery paths: swagger.json, openapi.json, api-docs, api/graphql, api/config, api/admin

entries.title: GraphQL field-level authorization
techniques: Treat introspection as mapping, not a finding. Evaluation for missing field-level auth, node/global ID object access, and mutation authorization gaps.
If introspection is blocked, use type suggestions, aliases, GET-vs-POST, content-type changes, and partial schema errors to discover fields.
Use aliases to test multiple object IDs in one request and compare attacker vs victim visibility with the same auth profile.
Check batching only where rate limits or OTP/login flows exist; record only if it enables practical bypass or account takeover.
payload_hints: { __schema { types { name fields { name type { name } } } } }
{ a1: user(id: 1) { id email } a2: user(id: 2) { id email } }
mutation { updateUserRole(userId: "VICTIM_ID", role: ADMIN) { id role } }
GET /graphql?query={__schema{types{name}}}

entries.title: WordPress REST, XML-RPC, and plugin action review
techniques: Enumerate REST users, author redirects, plugins, themes, uploads, debug logs, and backup config files, but only record when sensitive data or a real impact path exists.
For authenticated testing, use the lowest role available and test plugin admin-ajax actions for nonce-only auth, role confusion, upload, export, and privilege changes.
Treat xmlrpc.php as a signal for brute-force or pingback SSRF only when you can show bypassed controls or meaningful internal interaction.
Use plugin/theme names and versions as leads for impact research; banner disclosure alone is not reportable.
payload_hints: /wp-json/wp/v2/users and /?author=1
/wp-content/debug.log, /wp-config.php.bak, /wp-content/uploads/
POST /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with action=PLUGIN_ACTION as subscriber
POST /xmlrpc.php system.listMethods

entries.title: Next.js data, middleware, and image optimizer checks
techniques: Inspect __NEXT_DATA__ and /_next/data JSON for authenticated props, user data, feature flags, internal URLs, and stale admin data.
Test middleware bypass with /_next/data route equivalents for sensitive pages and compare auth behavior with the page route.
For /_next/image or rewrite-backed proxy endpoints, test only bounded SSRF paths that can prove internal fetch or cloud metadata access.
Server Actions and API routes often have inconsistent CSRF/origin checks; prioritize destructive or account-changing actions.
payload_hints: /_next/data/BUILD_ID/admin/dashboard.json
/_next/image?url=https://attacker.example/redirect-to-internal&w=128&q=75
Origin: null on Server Action POSTs
Search JS/config for rewrites to internal destinations

entries.title: JWT, OAuth, and OIDC token trust boundaries
techniques: For JWT, test claim trust only when the app accepts tampered tokens: alg none, RS256-to-HS256 confusion, kid path tricks, attacker JWKS, role/sub/email edits.
For OAuth/OIDC, chain open redirects into redirect_uri validation, state/nonce weakness, missing PKCE, token leakage in Referer, and scope escalation.
Confirm impact by reaching protected data or impersonating a role. A weak token shape without accepted forgery is not enough.
Check .well-known metadata and JWKS for algorithm/key confusion leads before attempting token attacks.
payload_hints: /.well-known/openid-configuration and /.well-known/jwks.json
redirect_uri=https://attacker.example/callback or encoded/scheme-relative variants
JWT header tests: alg=none, HS256 with public key, kid=../../../../dev/null
Scope expansion: scope=openid profile email admin offline_access

entries.title: SSRF and server-side URL fetch impact
techniques: DNS-only callbacks are lead data. Escalate to internal service access, cloud metadata, credential exfiltration, or internal admin reachability before recording.
Test redirect handling, parser confusion, IPv6/decimal/octal/hex loopback forms, and metadata hostnames only after confirming a server-side fetch.
Check second-order SSRF where a URL is saved first, then fetched by preview, import, PDF, avatar, or webhook processors.
Record exact request, callback evidence, and internal response evidence. Avoid high-volume port scans.
payload_hints: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token
External URL that 302 redirects to an internal URL

entries.title: Upload, file handling, and stored client-side execution
techniques: Check extension, MIME, magic-byte, path, and processing differentials. Look for stored XSS, parser bugs, overwrite, traversal, public object ACLs, and unsafe transforms.
For images/documents, test where uploaded content is rendered: user profile, admin moderation, PDF preview, email templates, and shared documents.
Do not report upload of inert files. Prove script execution, sensitive read/write, overwrite, or server-side processing impact.
If storage is cloud-backed, test signed URL scope, bucket listing, cross-tenant object access, and predictable object keys.
payload_hints: polyglot names: file.php.jpg, file.svg, file.html, file..jpg, file.jpg%00.php
SVG with harmless script proof for same-origin render checks
Content-Type mismatch: image/jpeg body with HTML/SVG, or form MIME vs file magic
Object key traversal/predictability: ../, tenant_id changes, sequential IDs

entries.title: Business logic and race-condition targets
techniques: Evaluation workflow skips, negative/zero values, stale state, privilege-changing invitation flows, plan upgrades, refunds, and credit transfers.
Race only critical state transitions: coupon redemption, credit spend, password reset, MFA verify, invite accept, checkout, refund, and file processing.
Compare UI-enforced constraints with direct API requests. The best bugs live where client steps imply server trust.
Record only if money, credits, access, inventory, or account state changes in a way a real attacker can use.
payload_hints: amount=-100, quantity=0, price=0, plan=premium with skipped payment step
20 parallel identical redemption or transfer requests against one balance/coupon
Replay invite or reset token after use; accept invite while changing role/org params
Switch attacker/victim IDs in checkout, subscription, refund, invoice, or export routes

entries.title: OSS dependency and supply-chain review
techniques: Review install/build/release scripts for shelling out to untrusted input, curl|bash patterns, token exposure, and publication-time surprises.
Compare manifests and lockfiles for unpinned dependencies, unexpected registry use, dependency confusion risk, and dangerous lifecycle scripts.
Treat known-vulnerable packages as findings only when the affected code path or reachable deployment mode is clear.
payload_hints: package.json scripts: preinstall, install, postinstall, prepare, publish, release
Lockfile drift: manifest dependency not represented in the lockfile
Registry hints: publishConfig.registry, .npmrc, pip index-url, go replace

entries.title: OSS native-code parser, protocol, and memory-safety review
techniques: Trace attacker-controlled packet, file, URL, or API input to the exact parser/state transition before recording a finding.
Review length-prefixed buffers, opaque fields, arrays, strings, and allocation-size math for signed/unsigned truncation, wraparound, and missing bounds checks.
For protocol clients, treat malicious server replies as attacker-controlled; for servers, treat client requests and exported paths as attacker-controlled.
Check ownership/lifetime around async callbacks, PDU queues, reference counters, and cleanup paths for use-after-free, double-free, and stale pointer use.
Prefer ASAN/UBSAN, existing unit tests, or a minimal harness command; if repro is blocked, record the exact build or input blocker instead of overstating proof.
payload_hints: Large XDR length: 0xffffffff or 0x80000000 against string/opaque/array fields
Boundary lengths: 0, 1, 3, 4, 7, 8, 1024, MAX_FRAGMENT_SIZE, MAX_FRAGMENT_SIZE+1
Path cases: embedded NUL, ../ traversal, absolute path, repeated slashes, long component, non-UTF8 bytes
Allocator cases: count * element_size overflow, size + header overflow, malloc/calloc failure path
Async lifetime: callback frees context while queued PDU or reconnect path still references it

entries.title: OSS auth, API, CI, and config review
techniques: Trace route handlers to auth middleware and policy checks; prioritize endpoints where docs imply protection but the code allows direct access.
Review CI workflows for pull_request_target misuse, untrusted checkout/script execution, broad token permissions, and secret exposure to forks.
Review config and env examples for unsafe defaults, required secrets without rotation guidance, and behavior that contradicts SECURITY/README docs.
payload_hints: GitHub Actions: pull_request_target plus checkout of attacker-controlled head ref
Middleware bypass: route group or API handler missing the auth wrapper used elsewhere
Config smell: default admin token, DEBUG=true, wildcard CORS, disabled CSRF
